question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: FINAL ANSWER: Answer — short summary - This looks like multiple related symptoms that are best treated as a coordinated multi‑system incident (not a single client fault). Some items point to tenant/identity or shared backend changes (D365 Financial Reports), and the telephony symptoms point to edge/SBC and site‑edge network changes (new hotline numbers / Basel ~30s drops). Teams banner suppression looks endpoint‑level (Focus Assist / Teams banner setting) but could be a GPO/tenant policy if widespread. Treat this as a 2nd‑level, cross‑team incident requiring Apps (D365), Identity, UC/SBC, Network and Endpoint teams to work together.

Activities 18.09.25 08:40 – Email from Contact Person 22 Good morning We have noticed that incoming calls from certain customers are no longer reaching us. Kind regards 18.09.25 08:56 Ticket created and assigned to Employee 41. 18.09.25 09:25 – Helpdesk inquiry Good day Could you please provide the following information: – Example phone numbers where the issue occurs – Since when the issue has existed – Whether all users are affected or only specific ones Thank you. 18.09.25 10:15 – Customer response Here are two examples: +41 44 785 23 10 +41 44 785 23 11 Other numbers such as +41 31 … work without issue. The problem has existed since last week. All employees are affected. 18.09.25 11:40 – Internal analysis Test call from private mobile (different number range) → works. Test with number in specified range → call not delivered. In the Teams Admin Center, no incoming call is shown. 19.09.25 09:05 – Initial hypothesis Possible provider issue. Carrier contacted. 19.09.25 14:20 – Carrier feedback From the carrier’s perspective, the call is correctly delivered to the company’s SBC. No routing errors visible on their side. 22.09.25 08:55 – SBC log analysis For functioning numbers: INVITE accepted and forwarded to Teams. For affected numbers: INVITE received but immediately answered with “404 Not Found”. 22.09.25 11:30 – Email to customer Good day We see that incoming calls from the provider are reaching the SBC. However, the requests are not being correctly forwarded internally. Were there recent changes to number ranges or new extensions? Kind regards 22.09.25 13:05 – Customer response Yes, we received a new number range from the provider last week and partially migrated old numbers. The affected numbers belong to this new range. 23.09.25 09:10 – Internal review Comparison with document “Blueprint – Microsoft Teams Direct Routing & Number Translation – Version 2.3” Section regarding: • Normalization of incoming numbers • E.164 format • Translation rules on SBC • Voice routing policies Finding: New numbers are not included in the SBC translation table. Routing rule covers only the old number range. 24.09.25 – Test Temporary adjustment of translation rule for new number range. Test call performed. Call correctly signaled and delivered. Reverting to original configuration → issue reproducible. 25.09.25 – Coordination with customer Confirmation that new number range was fully migrated. Internal documentation had not yet been updated. 26.09.25 – Adjustment Routing and translation rules extended according to architectural specification. Details not documented in ticket for security reasons. 29.09.25 – Customer feedback All tested numbers are working again. Multiple customers also confirmed successful calls. Thank you for the support. 29.09.25 16:18 – Ticket closure Dear Contact Person 22 The root cause was an inconsistent translation and routing configuration following the introduction of a new number range. The configuration was adjusted according to the internal Direct Routing architecture. Incoming calls are now correctly delivered again. Test call performed. Call remains stable for several minutes. UDP session timeout configured to 30 seconds. Comparison with Zurich location → 180 seconds. 29.07.25 – Adjustment NAT session timeout adjusted according to architecture specification. Details not documented in ticket for security reasons. 30.07.25 – Customer feedback Good morning We conducted several test calls. No more disconnections observed. Longer conversations are also stable. Thank you very much. 31.07.25 15:52 – Ticket closure Dear Contact Person 16 The root cause was a deviation in the session and NAT configuration at the Basel location related to the new VLAN structure. The configuration was adjusted according to the internal Direct Routing architecture. Calls now remain stable. answer: . This pattern in the records was associated with shared backend/metadata/auth issues (AOS/IIS/TLS, Data Mart/FRDB or identity/service account problems) rather than a single-client problem. Recommended evidence: AOS/app‑tier logs, IIS/Schannel events, FRDB/SQL errors and any recent tenant/identity changes.

If no improvement: open a Microsoft ticket. 27.03.24 17:02 | ApplicationUser, CRM Development | WEB Waiting REPORT 56: # Management Reporting & Financial Reporting Infrastructure Standard (D365) — Customer Company 11 An approved operational and architecture standard (Management Reporting & Financial Reporting Infrastructure Standard V1.0) defines the design, security configuration and operational procedures for the Financial Reporting stack used in Customer Company 11's D365 Finance & Operations environments (UAT and PROD). The standard names a small set of high-impact components and dependencies: the Financial Reporting Data Mart (aggregated SQL store), the SQL backend that hosts metadata, an IIS/TLS layer that enforces cipher suites, service accounts used by reporting components, and operational actions such as Data Mart resets and decision-tree guided troubleshooting. The decision-tree/use-cases section maps observable symptoms (for example, reports showing authors as “unknown”) to concrete checks (service account connectivity, TLS/cipher-suite compatibility, SQL connectivity, AOS service, IIS configuration) and prescribes synchronous, environment-consistent changes (for cipher suites, AOS restarts, and Data Mart resets) to avoid broken flows across reporting components. The document is specifically authored for Customer Company 11 and is the authoritative reference tying troubleshooting, configuration management and recovery actions together. ## Standard document is the central governance artifact for reporting infrastructure The Management Reporting & Financial Reporting Infrastructure Standard V1.0 is the controlling architecture and operational standard for the Financial Reporting components in Customer Company 11's D365 environment and explicitly applies to UAT and PROD environments, making it the authoritative source for configuration and runbook behavior [Data: Entities (476); Relationships (778)]. Because the decision-tree and use-case sections are embedded in the same document, the standard both prescribes expected runtime configuration (TLS/cipher-suite rules, service-account behavior) and enumerates the troubleshooting workflow that engineers must follow when incidents occur, tying governance to operations [Data: Entities (476, 484); Relationships (784)]. ## Financial Reporting Data Mart is a single, critical dependency for report availability and correctness The Financial Reporting Data Mart is described as an aggregated SQL-based store relied upon by the Report Designer and Financial Reporting Service; its consistency is essential to correct report results and availability, which makes it a primary dependency in incident investigations [Data: Entities (478); Relationships (792)]. Operational guidance in the standard endorses a Data Mart reset as a remediation when data inconsistency is isolated to a single environment, indicating that the Data Mart is both a frequent root-cause locus and a recoverable subsystem when handled according to the runbook [Data: Entities (489); Relationships (795)]. ## SQL backend is the metadata authority and first-line troubleshooting target Report metadata and authorship information are stored and retrieved via the SQL backend/Data Mart; therefore SQL connectivity and database health directly affect metadata visibility and report rendering [Data: Entities (482, 485); Relationships (807)]. The decision-tree explicitly includes SQL connection checks as a primary step when reports or data are unavailable, making SQL health an early and repeatable check in incident triage workflows [Data: Entities (484, 482); Relationships (806)]. ## IIS/TLS layer and cipher-suite configuration are high-risk failure points for authentication and metadata issues An IIS-based TLS termination layer enforces TLS and cipher-suite settings for the reporting infrastructure; the standard highlights that misconfiguration here can block or degrade report access and functionality by breaking TLS negotiation between clients and backend components [Data: Entities (479, 486); Relationships (800, 802)]. The decision tree links TLS/cipher-suite incompatibility to observable symptoms such as report authors shown as “unknown,” indicating that TLS failures can manifest as metadata/authentication failures rather than obvious transport-level errors, and therefore must be included in runbooks and monitoring thresholds [Data: Relationships (804); Entities (491, 486)]. ## Service account connectivity and configuration are primary causes of missing author metadata The standard calls out service accounts as the identities used by reporting components to access the Data Mart and metadata; when report authors appear as “unknown,” the runbook specifically prescribes checking service account connectivity and permissions as a primary diagnostic action [Data: Entities (481, 491); Relationships (792, 805)]. This linkage makes service-account lifecycle controls (password/credential rotation, consistent configuration across environments) and monitoring of account authentication health operational priorities to avoid metadata loss and degraded author attribution [Data: Entities (481); Relationships (805)]. ## Decision tree maps symptoms to concrete component checks and environment-consistent change rules The document section containing decision trees and use cases maps specific symptoms to component checks (Data Mart reset, SQL connection, AOS service, IIS configuration, cipher-suite/TLS checks), providing an actionable troubleshooting workflow that ties observable issues to discrete remediation steps [Data: Entities (484); Relationships (784, 806)]. The standard further prescribes operational principles such as synchronizing cipher-suite changes across environments and performing AOS restarts after certain adjustments, which reduce the risk of asymmetric changes causing environment-specific failures; these procedural constraints should be enforced by change control and deployment automation [Data: Entities (476); Relationships (784)]. ## Recurring failure mode pattern: TLS/cipher mismatches and service-account authentication produce metadata anomalies The provided artifacts explicitly link TLS/cipher-suite incompatibility to both transport-level failures and the symptom of unknown report authors, and they separately identify service-account authentication problems as a cause of the same symptom, demonstrating a common failure-mode cluster where authentication/crypto misconfiguration and identity issues produce indistinct metadata anomalies [Data: Relationships (804, 805); Entities (486, 481, 491)]. Because multiple components (IIS/TLS layer, SQL backend/Data Mart, service accounts) can manifest the same operational symptom, the decision-tree's ordered checks (service accounts, TLS, SQL, Data Mart reset) should be codified into runbooks and automated diagnostics to avoid repeated firefighting and to direct engineers to the most-likely root cause quickly [Data: Entities (484, 478, 482); Relationships (792, 806, 795)]. ## Operational recovery guidance exists but relies on environment consistency and synchronous changes The standard contains explicit instructions that certain configuration changes (for example, allowed cipher suites) be synchronized across environments and that AOS restarts and Data Mart resets be used in a controlled manner; this indicates that successful recovery depends on disciplined change management and environment parity [Data: Entities (476, 486, 489); Relationships (784, 795)]. Absent automated enforcement of environment parity and pre-change compatibility checks, there is risk that asynchronous or partial rollouts (e.g., different cipher suites in UAT vs PROD) will create intermittent, environment-specific failures; the document's direction therefore implies the need for gating changes through the standard's decision-tree and CI/CD controls [Data: Entities (476, 486); Relationships (784)]. ## Controls and monitoring gaps implied by the document should be prioritized for remediation The artifact set highlights the criticality of TLS/cipher configuration, SQL/Data Mart health, and service-account authentication but does not itself include explicit monitoring/alert thresholds or automated remediation steps in the extracted records; given these components' operational impact, establishing targeted alerts for TLS negotiation failures, SQL connectivity errors, service-account authentication failures, and Data Mart consistency checks should be a near-term priority [Data: Entities (479, 482, 481, 478); Relationships (800, 806, 792, 795)]. Formalizing these checks into runbooks and integrating them with the decision-tree/use-cases in the standard (for example, automated pre-flight checks before cipher-suite changes and automated Data Mart consistency verification) will reduce time-to-detect and time-to-recover for the recurring failure modes documented here [Data: Entities (484, 476); Relationships (784)]. REPORT 25: # NDES / AD CS Certificate Enrollment Community (NDES, Issuing CA, Templates, Devices) This community centers on the Network Device Enrollment Service (NDES) as the operational bridge that automates certificate issuance from Active Directory Certificate Services (AD CS) to target devices (network appliances and managed endpoints). NDES depends directly on AD CS (Issuing CA and certificate templates) and on AD CS post-configuration steps being completed; it also integrates with Intune and targets both network devices and managed endpoints for automated enrollment. The Issuing CA and Certificate Templates are critical configuration points: misconfigured templates or incorrect CA selection commonly cause issuance failures, while Root CA and Issuing CA operational problems (expiry or mismanagement) break certificate validation across consumers. Key operational failure modes called out in the corpus include missing IIS components or NDES service account misconfiguration, incomplete AD CS post-configuration, and template/CA misconfiguration; the decision-tree used for troubleshooting explicitly gates issuance testing on AD CS post-configuration and template correctness. [Data: Entities (360, 364, 365, 370, 369, 375, 376, 363), Relationships (584, 589, 590, 592, 594, 595, 601, 602)] ## NDES is the central operational component connecting AD CS to device consumers NDES functions as the active enrollment service that requests certificates from AD CS and provisions them to devices and managed endpoints; this makes it the focal point for certificate-based device authentication in the environment [Data: Entities (360), Relationships (584, 590, 595)]. Because NDES both initiates requests (to the Issuing CA using configured templates) and delivers certificates to targets (network devices and managed endpoints), failures in NDES manifest as downstream authentication and connectivity problems for many device types—making timely detection and remediation critical for device management and network security posture [Data: Relationships (589, 594, 595)]. ## Common NDES failure modes are documented and actionable The NDES description explicitly lists operational failure modes including inability to start the service, missing IIS components, service-account misconfiguration, AD CS post-configuration errors, and broken communication between NDES and the CA; these are primary troubleshooting paths to investigate when issuance fails [Data: Entities (360)]. The decision-tree logic in the blueprint enforces verification steps (for example, checking AD CS post-configuration) before attempting issuance tests, which both documents these frequent failure causes and provides an operational sequence for root-cause isolation [Data: Entities (360), Relationships (592)]. ## Certificate template misconfiguration is a frequent, high-impact root cause Certificate templates define key usage, validity, and subject fields for issued certificates and are explicitly called out as a common cause of failed automated issuance when misconfigured; the blueprint recommends documentation and regular review of templates to prevent these issues [Data: Entities (365)]. Operationally, templates are consumed by the Issuing CA to produce end-entity certificates; if templates are incorrect on the CA side or selected incorrectly by NDES, issuance will fail or produce certificates incompatible with device expectations, causing authentication errors across the environment [Data: Relationships (602, 589)]. ## CA hierarchy (Root CA → Issuing CA) is a single point of trust and failure The Root CA is the trust anchor that signs the Issuing CA certificate; therefore issues at the Root CA or Issuing CA (expiry, mismanagement, incorrect chaining) can break certificate validation for all issued certificates and impact broad sets of devices and services [Data: Entities (363, 364), Relationships (601)]. Because NDES must be configured to use the correct Issuing CA, any mismatch between NDES configuration and the operational Issuing CA (or problems with the CA chain) will prevent successful enrollment and cause widespread authentication failures until corrected [Data: Relationships (584, 602)]. ## Network devices and managed endpoints are critical targets and verification points The blueprint identifies network appliances (routers, switches, firewalls, VPN gateways) and Intune-managed endpoints as primary recipients of NDES-issued certificates; these device classes should be part of any enrollment verification and post-change validation tests [Data: Entities (375, 376, 370), Relationships (594, 595, 590)]. Because successful issuance is verified by testing enrollment on targeted devices as the final decision-tree step, operational runbooks and monitoring should include sample enrollments against representative device models and managed endpoint profiles to detect regressions early [Data: Relationships (590)]. ## AD CS post-configuration is a gating dependency for NDES operation The corpus explicitly states that AD CS post-configuration (registration of NDES components, enabling required templates, selecting the correct Issuing CA) must be completed before proceeding to issuance tests; the decision tree checks this as a distinct step, making it a documented dependency that must be verified during deployments and troubleshooting [Data: Entities (369), Relationships (592)]. Operational processes should therefore include a checklist for AD CS post-configuration sign-off as a prerequisite for NDES activation and for any change that affects templates or CA selection to avoid wasted troubleshooting effort and false negatives during enrollment testing [Data: Entities (369), Relationships (592)]. ## Recommended operational controls: template governance, CA lifecycle management, and NDES platform hardening The dataset highlights template documentation and regular review as mitigations against issuance failures; implementing template governance (versioning, approvals, change logging) directly addresses a repeatedly-cited root cause [Data: Entities (365)]. Similarly, because Root CA and Issuing CA issues break validation widely, organizations need CA lifecycle controls (expiry monitoring, dedicated owners, offline Root CA handling procedures) to reduce systemic risk; these controls are supported by the Root CA and Issuing CA descriptions that call out expiry and mismanagement as service-impacting events [Data: Entities (363, 364), Relationships (601)]. Finally, hardening and operational checks for NDES—ensuring correct IIS components, validating the NDES service account, and maintaining reliable connectivity between NDES and the Issuing CA—map directly to the NDES failure modes and should be codified in installation and runbook procedures [Data: Entities (360)]. ## Suggested stakeholder/escalation pathway derived from dependency graph The relationship map shows NDES depending on AD CS (Issuing CA and templates) and provisioning certificates to devices, which implies a practical escalation sequence: NDES operations (first responder) → Issuing CA/AD CS administrators (certificate/template investigations) → Root CA owners (if trust-chain/expiry issues) → device owners (network or endpoint teams) for remediation and verification [Data: Entities (360, 364, 363, 370), Relationships (584, 601, 590)]. Making this path explicit in runbooks will shorten mean-time-to-resolution by directing incidents to the correct technical owner quickly—particularly for issues like template misconfiguration or CA chain problems that require CA-side changes rather than device-side fixes [Data: Relationships (589, 602, 590)].

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 74: # SBC Translation Table / Direct Routing — Missing New Number Range This community centers on the PSTN-to-Teams Direct Routing chain: the Carrier/Provider delivers calls to the customer's SBC, the SBC translation table implements the Routing and Translation Rules (including E.164 normalization), and those rules feed Voice Routing Policies that route calls to Teams. During a recent migration the carrier provisioned a New Number Range which was partially migrated by the customer; those new numbers (+41 44 785 23 10/11 are given as examples) were not present in the SBC translation table and associated routing rules, causing INVITEs for that range to be mis-answered (404) and not forwarded to Teams. The carrier confirmed upstream delivery to the SBC, helping isolate the failure to SBC-side translation/routing configuration rather than upstream provisioning. [Data: Entities (177,178,179,180,181,183), Relationships (274,276,278,279,282,275)] ## Primary technical root cause: missing new-range entries in the SBC translation table Investigation identified that new telephone numbers provisioned by the carrier were not present in the SBC translation table, so INVITEs for that range were mis-answered (404) and not forwarded into Teams. This indicates a configuration gap where the concrete SBC translation entries did not include the new number range(s) introduced during migration [Data: Entities (178,183), Relationships (276,282)]. Because the SBC translation table is the concrete implementation of the Routing and Translation Rules, an omission at that configuration artifact directly prevented normalization and mapping for the affected numbers, breaking the call delivery chain to Teams until the entries were added to the table. This is consistent with the relationships tying the translation table to routing rules and the old/new range contrast observed during troubleshooting [Data: Entities (178,179,183), Relationships (276,282)]. ## Carrier confirmation isolates failure to SBC-side processing (not upstream delivery) The carrier/provider reported that they delivered calls correctly to the SBC and saw no routing errors on their side, which helped rule out upstream delivery issues and focus the investigation on SBC processing and configuration [Data: Entities (177), Relationships (274)]. Operationally, this eliminates carrier routing as the immediate cause and shortens the troubleshooting path to SBC translation and normalization logic, which should be the primary focus for immediate remediation and validation steps. The existing carrier confirmation should be recorded in the incident timeline and retained as evidence for post-incident review and change verification [Data: Relationships (274)]. ## Dependency chain: Routing & Translation Rules → E.164 normalization → Voice Routing Policies The components form a linear dependency chain where Routing and Translation Rules implement E.164 normalization and feed normalized numbers into Voice Routing Policies that determine Teams delivery; failures within the translation layer therefore propagate downstream to voice routing and call delivery [Data: Relationships (278,279), Entities (179,181,180)]. This chain highlights a single-point-of-impact: while multiple components participate in call delivery, the translation table acts as the authoritative mapping layer. Any gaps or misconfigurations in the translation table or the translation rules will prevent normalized address generation and thus prevent voice routing policies from matching and delivering calls to Teams users or voicemail/auto-attendants [Data: Entities (178,179,180)]. ## Migration and change-control gap: new range was provisioned but translation rules were not updated The new number range was assigned/provisioned by the carrier and partially migrated by the customer the week prior to the issue, creating a requirement to update SBC translation rules that was not met prior to cutover; the relationship metadata explicitly links the migration to the need to update SBC entries [Data: Entities (183), Relationships (275)]. This sequence suggests a process gap between telecommunications provisioning and SBC configuration change (e.g., missing checklist item, absent automated provisioning, or an incomplete change request). That control gap materially increased the likelihood of service disruption for the new range and should be addressed by process and governance changes (see recommendations). ## Observed failure mode: INVITEs mis-answered with 404 and not forwarded to Teams The direct symptom reported was that INVITEs for numbers in the new range were mis-answered with 404 responses and were not forwarded into Teams, consistent with missing or mismatched normalization/mapping entries on the SBC that prevented routing policies from matching the calls to internal destinations [Data: Entities (178,183), Relationships (276,282)]. This failure mode is reproducible and actionable: adding the correct normalization and translation entries for the new range should return correct call handling and prevent 404 mis-answers for those numbers. As such, verification steps can be scripted into a runbook for validation after updates are applied. ## Immediate remediation priorities and validation steps The highest-priority corrective action is to add the new-number-range entries to the SBC translation table and ensure the corresponding routing/translation rules implement correct E.164 normalization so the Voice Routing Policies match the calls to Teams endpoints [Data: Entities (178,179,181), Relationships (276,279,278)]. Follow-up validation should include (a) inbound test calls from the carrier for affected sample numbers, (b) SBC logs verifying correct translation and normalization, (c) verification that calls reach Teams and that no 404s are returned, and (d) a change-control record that ties the carrier provisioning event to the SBC configuration change to close the loop. Retain the carrier’s confirmation of upstream delivery as supporting evidence [Data: Entities (177,183), Relationships (274,275)]. ## Operational risk and recurrence: process and monitoring deficiencies The root cause and migration timeline point to a repeatable operational risk: when carriers provision new ranges, failure to propagate those ranges into SBC translation rules will repeatedly cause call delivery failures. The presence of an existing correctly functioning old number range makes such omissions less obvious until customers or users on the new range report failures [Data: Entities (184,183), Relationships (282)]. Mitigations should include: a) formalized handoff checklist between carrier provisioning and SBC configuration owners, b) automated reconciliation that compares carrier-provided ranges to SBC translation table contents, c) monitoring/alerting for abnormal 404 rates on the SBC for inbound PSTN INVITEs, and d) test call validation as part of cutover acceptance. ## Stakeholder mapping and escalation pathway Key stakeholders in this community are the Carrier/Provider (responsible for PSTN provisioning and initial delivery), the SBC owner/telephony/networking team (responsible for translation tables and routing rules), and the Teams/UC platform team (consumer of normalized addresses via Voice Routing Policies). The relationships show direct handoffs from Carrier → SBC and SBC configuration → Voice Routing Policies that affect Teams delivery [Data: Entities (177,178,180,179), Relationships (274,278)]. Escalation should follow: (1) carrier confirms delivery (already done) to quickly isolate the issue, (2) SBC/telephony team applies translation updates and notifies Teams/UC team, (3) Teams/UC validates call delivery and closes the loop, and (4) change-control and post-incident review document the migration and corrective steps for governance and audit. REPORT 76: # Direct Routing / SBC Incident: TI-1612036 — Customer Company 52 AG This community centers on a production Microsoft Teams Direct Routing incident (TI-1612036) opened for Customer Company 52 AG where inbound calls from specific PSTN numbers failed to be delivered. Investigation by the Unified Communications / Network team and Employee 41 showed SIP signaling and SDP negotiation succeeded but RTP was one-way: Microsoft-originating RTP reached the SBC while client-originating RTP did not consistently reach it. The carrier confirmed delivery to the SIP trunk/SBC, and a temporary SBC translation/normalization rule that included the new number range produced successful test calls; when reverted, the problem returned. Remediation consisted of adjusting SBC translation/routing rules per the Direct Routing blueprint and validating the fix, then closing the ticket after successful tests. Key components engaged were the SBC, Teams Admin Center/PowerShell for diagnostics, the SIP trunk, the carrier, Employee 41 as ticket owner, and the Unified Communications / Network team as technical owner. [Data: Entities (171, 114, 175, 173, 186, 185, 440); Relationships (257, 178, 731, 732, 266, 267, 268)] ## Primary cause: SBC translation/normalization rules omitted the new number range, blocking inbound call delivery mapping The incident was reproducibly resolved by adding a translation/normalization rule that included the new number range; temporary rule tests returned successful calls and reverting the change reproduced the failure, indicating the missing/insufficient translation rule on the SBC was the primary delivery failure mode rather than transport or carrier delivery. This behavior is documented in the ticket narrative and the temporary translation rule entity. [Data: Entities (171, 186); Relationships (267, 266)] The carrier confirmed calls reached the SIP trunk and were delivered to the SBC, which helped exclude carrier-side transport as the root cause and focused the investigation on SBC-side handling (translation/routing) and mapping logic. That confirmation is captured in the SIP trunk and coordination relationship. [Data: Entities (185); Relationships (268)] ## Media-path anomaly (one-way audio) was observed and is distinct from the delivery problem SBC logs and analysis showed successful SIP signaling and SDP negotiation while RTP was asymmetric: Microsoft-originating RTP was visible to the SBC but client-originating RTP was not consistently seen, producing one-way audio for affected calls. These observations separate signaling success from media failures and were central to troubleshooting. [Data: Entities (114, 453, 440); Relationships (731, 732, 179)] The SBC description explicitly notes common causes for such media asymmetry (client-side NAT/firewall blocking, asymmetric routing, RTP pinholing issues, or media-anchoring misconfiguration), which frames follow-on checks for network path, NAT policies, and SBC media handling if one-way issues recur. These are documented diagnostic expectations from the SBC analysis. [Data: Entities (114)] ## Investigation workflow and stakeholders were clear: Employee 41 led triage and coordinated with carrier and Unified Communications team Employee 41 was the ticket owner who coordinated troubleshooting activities, performed SBC log review and tests, applied the temporary translation rule, and engaged the carrier regarding the SIP trunk. The record of Employee 41 making translation-table adjustments and coordinating with the carrier is explicit in the dataset. [Data: Entities (173); Relationships (266, 267, 268)] The Unified Communications / Network team performed SBC/media and firewall analysis as the technical escalation path for Direct Routing and media flow, confirming the organizational escalation and ownership model during this incident. This team’s involvement is documented in the relationships and entity entries. [Data: Entities (113); Relationships (178)] ## Observability: Teams Admin Center and PowerShell provided complementary diagnostics but showed gaps for the affected numbers Teams Admin Center was used to inspect incoming call activity, Direct Routing configuration, and SBC health; it showed no incoming call entries for the affected numbers, which helped narrow diagnostics. Teams PowerShell was used for deeper tenant-level configuration retrieval when needed. This combination was effective but highlights a visibility gap for specific inbound-number telemetry in the Admin Center UI. [Data: Entities (175); Relationships (734, 838)] Because the Admin Center did not show incoming call activity for those numbers, investigators relied on SBC logs and carrier confirmation—indicating that runbooks should prescribe parallel checks (SBC logs + carrier trace + Teams diagnostics) for inbound-number failures to avoid diagnostic blind spots. [Data: Entities (175, 114); Relationships (732, 268)] ## Scope and impact: customer-facing inbound telephony disruption for specific PSTN numbers The ticket was opened on behalf of Customer Company 52 AG and included example affected numbers provided by the contact person, establishing a scoped customer-impact incident (not a tenant-wide outage but affecting inbound reachability from a particular number range). The ticket timeline shows the incident was opened 18.09.25 and closed 29.09.25 after validation. [Data: Entities (171, 172); Relationships (257, 258)] Operational impact was high for the affected call flows (callers experienced busy/disconnect), requiring cross-team coordination and temporary configuration changes to validate the permanent fix, indicating this type of failure has direct customer-journey consequences even if limited to a subset of numbers. [Data: Entities (171)] ## Evidence chain supports corrective action but suggests need for permanent change control and verification A temporary translation rule was used successfully to validate that including the new number range corrected delivery, and permanent routing/translation adjustments were applied per blueprint before closing the ticket—this sequence (test with temporary rule, then permanent update) demonstrates sound validation practice. The incident records explicitly describe the temporary rule producing successful tests and being reverted to reproduce the problem prior to the permanent update. [Data: Entities (186, 171); Relationships (267, 266)] Recommendation: codify this validated test-and-promote pattern into a change control and runbook (temporary rule test -> carrier confirmation -> promote to permanent translation/routing change -> post-change monitoring) to reduce risk of regressions and provide audit trails for future similar issues. The data supports this lifecycle as effective in this case. [Data: Entities (186, 173); Relationships (266, 267)] ## Recurring risk vectors remain: NAT/firewall RTP pinholing and SBC media handling should be monitored Although translation rules were the confirmed delivery fault in this incident, the SBC analysis also called out client NAT/firewall blocking, asymmetric routing, or RTP pinholing as common causes of one-way media—meaning that even after rule changes, media-path vulnerabilities could manifest under different conditions. The SBC entity explicitly lists these potential causes. [Data: Entities (114)] Operational mitigations should include RTP path monitoring, NAT traversal health checks, firewall policy reviews (for pinned RTP port ranges), and SBC configuration audits to detect media-anchoring or symmetric-routing deviations before they impact customers. These measures align with the observed media anomalies in the SBC logs. [Data: Entities (114, 440); Relationships (731, 732)] ## Recommended governance and runbook updates to prevent recurrence and accelerate resolution Based on the incident evidence, prioritized changes include: (1) enforce a change-control step requiring test with temporary translation rule and carrier confirmation before permanent promotion; (2) add inbound-number-range discovery and translation-table validation to routine SBC configuration audits; (3) improve Teams Admin Center telemetry correlation for inbound number traces or document explicit dependency on SBC/carrier logs in runbooks; and (4) maintain an incident playbook assigning Employee 41/UC team roles for Direct Routing incidents. These recommendations follow directly from the observed investigation steps, tools used, and the successful remediation pattern. [Data: Entities (173, 113, 175, 186); Relationships (266, 267, 178, 734)] Additionally, include periodic drills that simulate missing translation entries and one-way RTP scenarios to verify monitoring, alerting, and cross-team coordination (carrier, SIP trunk owner, UC team) operate as expected. The incident’s reproducible test-and-revert sequence demonstrates that such drills are feasible and informative. [Data: Entities (186, 171); Relationships (267)]

context: SOURCE 16: ﻿ Ticket summary: Ticket name: KF52: Microsoft Teams – External calls disconnect after approx. 30 seconds Ticket number: TI-1604879 Company: Customer Company 52 AG Contact person: Contact Person 16 Owner: Employee 35 Origin: Email Type: Incident Created on: 22.07.25 09:14 Closure Closure date: 31.07.25 15:52 Closure text: Analysis completed. Activities 22.07.25 09:02 – Email from Contact Person 16 to Helpdesk Good morning Since yesterday, several employees report that external calls in Microsoft Teams disconnect after approximately 30 seconds. The call is established normally, both parties can hear each other, but the connection is terminated after a short time. Internal Teams calls are not affected. Could you please review this? Kind regards 22.07.25 09:14 Ticket created and assigned to Employee 35. 22.07.25 10:05 – Helpdesk inquiry Good day Thank you for your report. Could you please confirm: – Does it affect incoming and outgoing calls? – Does the disconnection always occur after approximately the same duration? – Are all locations affected or only a specific one? Thank you very much. 22.07.25 10:42 – Customer response It only affects the Basel location. Both incoming and outgoing calls. The disconnection occurs almost exactly after 30 seconds, sometimes slightly earlier. Home office users are not affected. 22.07.25 11:30 – Internal note Test performed with affected user. External call to mobile phone. Call disconnected after 29–31 seconds. No error message in the Teams client, simply “Call Ended.” 23.07.25 08:55 – Initial hypothesis Possible client error or unstable connection. Test with another device → same behavior. Test with LAN cable instead of docking station → no change. 23.07.25 11:10 – Email to customer Good day We are currently analyzing the Direct Routing logs. Signaling is established correctly. The disconnection does not appear to be triggered by the client. Were there any recent network changes at the Basel location? Kind regards 23.07.25 13:45 – Customer response On Friday, a new network segment for VoIP traffic was implemented. A separate VLAN for “Voice” was created. Could this be relevant? 24.07.25 09:05 – Internal analysis SBC logs reviewed: – SIP INVITE successful – 200 OK correct – Call established – After 30 seconds, BYE from SBC No obvious error code. 24.07.25 10:30 – Firewall analysis No blocking visible. Ports open according to standard configuration. 24.07.25 14:20 – Detailed SIP analysis Session-Expires header reviewed. Session timer set to 30 seconds. Comparison with other locations shows: There, 180 seconds. 25.07.25 08:50 – Review of internal documentation Document: “Blueprint – Microsoft Teams Direct Routing & Session Handling – Version 2.1” Section on NAT session persistence and SIP session timers relevant. Note in the blueprint: For certain firewall or NAT configurations, the session timer must be adjusted to avoid premature termination. 25.07.25 11:15 – Internal tests Temporary adjustment of session parameters on SBC. Test call performed. Call remains stable for several minutes. Reverting to original configuration → disconnection after approx. 30 seconds reproducible. 28.07.25 – Coordination with network team New “Voice” VLAN uses different NAT policy. UDP session timeout configured to 30 seconds. Comparison with Zurich location → 180 seconds. 29.07.25 – Adjustment NAT session timeout adjusted according to architecture specification. The configuration was adjusted according to the internal Direct Routing architecture. Calls now remain stable. Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 75: # Basel SBC — 30s Session Timer / Voice VLAN NAT Timeout The community centers on an on‑premises Session Border Controller (SBC) handling carrier SIP calls into Microsoft Teams. SBC logs and test calls show a consistent symptom: external calls are established (INVITE → 200 OK) but are terminated by a BYE from the SBC at ~29–31 seconds, producing a "Call Ended" message in the Teams client. Investigation identified two linked contributors: (1) SIP Session-Expires headers in the affected Basel environment were set to 30 seconds (vs. 180s in other locations), and (2) NAT/UDP session timeouts applied on the new Voice VLAN caused sessions to be dropped despite signaling expectations. The SBC also exhibited split behavior by called number — some numbers were forwarded correctly while others were effectively answered with a 404 Not Found — pointing to SBC translation/routing rules impacting a subset of numbers. Temporary SBC parameter adjustments prevented the short disconnect during testing and reverting them reproduced the failure, validating the SBC/NAT-session timeout interaction as the root cause. ## SBC-initiated BYE at ~30 seconds is the immediate cause of call teardown Troubleshooting evidence shows the SBC sent a BYE at approximately 29–31 seconds which terminated calls even though INVITE and 200 OK exchanges had occurred, and this BYE correlated with the user-visible "Call Ended" message in the Teams client. This SBC-originated BYE is the direct observable cause of the premature disconnect across reproduced test calls. [Data: Entities (182,334,337); Relationships (524,545)] ## Session-Expires / SIP session timers are set to 30s in the affected Basel setup (vs. 180s elsewhere) The investigation identified that SIP Session-Expires (session timer) values in the affected Basel environment were observed at 30 seconds compared with 180 seconds in other locations, which materially changed expected refresh behavior and contributed to premature session termination. SIP session timers (Session-Expires header) were reviewed as part of the root-cause analysis and were directly linked to observed SIP message timing. [Data: Entities (324,323); Relationships (528,531)] ## Voice VLAN NAT/UDP timeouts caused sessions to be dropped despite SIP signaling Network-level NAT/UDP session timeouts applied to the new Voice VLAN caused effective session lifetimes to be truncated (a 30s NAT timeout was noted), dropping sessions even where SIP signaling suggested a longer lifetime; this created a mismatch between protocol-level timer expectations and network-level state. The Voice VLAN NAT behavior was identified as a contributing factor that allowed short-lived sessions to be torn down independently of SIP protocol errors. [Data: Entities (324); Relationships (530)] ## SBC translation/routing caused split behavior by destination number (forward vs 404) SBC logs showed differing SIP behavior by number: for functioning numbers the SBC accepted and forwarded the INVITE to Teams, while for affected numbers the SBC effectively answered with a 404 Not Found. This split indicates an SBC configuration/translation or routing table issue that affected a subset of numbers, localizing a secondary failure mode to SBC number handling logic rather than to the carrier or Teams. [Data: Entities (182,176); Relationships (283)] ## Temporary SBC parameter adjustments validated the SBC/NAT timeout interaction as root cause Temporary changes to SBC session parameters during testing prevented the disconnects, and reverting those changes reproduced the ~30s failure. These controlled parameter adjustments were used to validate that altering SBC session handling could mitigate the issue prior to making permanent changes. This provides a solid reproducible proof-of-fix methodology. [Data: Entities (329,176); Relationships (527)] ## Test calls reliably reproduce the symptom and correlate client UI with SBC logs Test calls to mobile phones consistently produced the client-side "Call Ended" message after ~29–31 seconds, and those test call records correlated to SBC logs showing the BYE and the earlier INVITE/200 OK exchange, giving a reliable, observable reproduction path for troubleshooting and verification of fixes. This correlation supports both operational verification and post-change validation steps. [Data: Entities (330,337,182); Relationships (544,524)] ## Two-layer failure model: protocol timer vs network state requires coordinated remediation The problem is best described as a two-layer interaction: (a) SIP session timer values (Session-Expires) govern protocol-level refresh expectations, and (b) network NAT/UDP session timeouts on the Voice VLAN determine whether refresh packets can be carried successfully. Because NAT expiry was shorter than SIP refresh expectations (or SIP values were reduced to 30s), sessions were being torn down prematurely at the network layer even when signaling indicated an active call. Effective remediation therefore requires changes both at SBC/session-timer configuration and at Voice VLAN NAT/UDP timeout settings. [Data: Entities (324,323); Relationships (528,530,531)] ## Operational evidence and artifacts exist to create targeted runbooks and monitoring SBC logs, SIP message traces, and reproducible test calls provide clear artifacts that can be used to build a runbook: steps to reproduce, log locations to check (SBC), key SIP headers to inspect (Session-Expires), and client-side symptom verification. The presence of reproducible tests and validated temporary mitigations means a playbook can include an immediate mitigation (temporary SBC params), verification steps (test calls and log correlation), and post-change validation. These artifacts should be codified into change and incident runbooks to reduce mean time to repair on recurrence. [Data: Entities (182,329,330,337); Relationships (527,544,524)]

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 46: # Endpoint Teams Notification Failure — Laptop (Teams banner + Focus Assist + Windows Update) The incident centers on a single user's laptop where Teams desktop banner (pop-up) notifications were not appearing. Troubleshooting found two co-occurring causes: the Teams notification channel (banner) was disabled in Teams settings and Windows Focus Assist (an OS feature) was active on the laptop, which together prevented toast/pop-up notifications from surfacing. The Windows Notification Center is the OS component responsible for surfacing app notifications and was validated during blueprint-driven triage. A recent Windows Update on the affected laptop was recorded and considered as a proximate event that might have changed OS behavior or settings, and thus is part of the root-cause narrative. ## Root cause: combined application and OS-level suppression of notifications The immediate functional cause of missing Teams pop-up notifications was the combination of the Teams banner being disabled and Windows Focus Assist being active on the endpoint. The dataset explicitly notes the Teams notification channel (banner) was disabled in the user's Teams settings and that Focus Assist on the laptop was blocking Teams pop-ups; both conditions were present in the incident [Data: Entities (380, 381); Relationships (625, 626)]. Windows Focus Assist can suppress notifications even when an app's own notification channel is enabled — the relationship data captures that interaction and confirms the dual-contribution to the symptom in this case (banner disabled + Focus Assist active) [Data: Relationships (625, 626)]. ## Windows Notification Center role and blueprint-driven checks were performed The Windows Notification Center is the OS component that surfaces app notifications and was specifically referenced as part of the blueprint verification performed during triage [Data: Entities (382)]. Relationship records indicate the Notification Center on the laptop was checked as part of troubleshooting, confirming that the runbook/blueprint included OS-level verification steps during the investigation [Data: Relationships (628)]. ## Device and update linkage: the issue was observed on a single laptop but tied to a recent Windows Update The incident manifested only on the user's laptop (the affected endpoint) and the dataset identifies the laptop as the location where notifications were blocked [Data: Entities (383)]. A recent Windows Update was recorded as having run on the affected laptop the week before the incident and was explicitly considered during triage as a proximate event that could explain changed notification behavior; relationship records link the Windows Update activity to the laptop and to the broader Windows OS context used during analysis [Data: Entities (386, 385); Relationships (630, 631)]. ## Change/patch events increase recurrence risk when they alter OS notification settings The presence of a recent Windows Update tied to the laptop suggests that OS patching or updates can be a trigger for altered endpoint settings (either resetting user preferences or changing default behaviors). The dataset identifies the update as part of the root-cause sequence and as a factor considered during triage, indicating that updates are a credible vector for recurrence [Data: Entities (386); Relationships (631)]. Because updates are regularly applied across fleets, similar symptoms could reappear on other endpoints if updates modify notification-related settings or if Focus Assist defaults differ after patching. ## Operational impact is localized but affects user productivity and notification-dependent workflows The incident was limited to a single endpoint (the user's laptop) rather than a tenant-wide Teams outage, indicating low immediate impact on overall service availability but measurable impact on the affected user's ability to receive timely Teams alerts [Data: Entities (383, 380)]. Even localized notification failures can affect responsiveness for users relying on desktop pop-ups for collaboration or incident response; therefore, while not a broad outage, the issue has tangible productivity implications that justify runbook updates and targeted preventative controls. ## Existing blueprint and triage steps captured key OS components but should be formalized into checklist items The dataset shows the blueprint included checking the Windows Notification Center during troubleshooting, which demonstrates good practice in verifying OS-level notification plumbing [Data: Entities (382); Relationships (628)]. However, the co-occurrence of Teams banner setting and Focus Assist implies the troubleshooting flow should explicitly list those controls (Teams notification channel, Focus Assist state, Notification Center, and recent Windows Update history) as discrete checklist items to ensure consistent diagnosis and remediation across similar incidents. ## Priority recommendations: triage/runbook updates, endpoint policy controls, and monitoring of update-induced changes Recommended near-term actions: update the endpoint support runbook to require verification of (a) Teams notification channel (banner) state, (b) Windows Focus Assist state, (c) Windows Notification Center app permissions, and (d) recent Windows Update history when users report missing desktop notifications. The dataset supports the need to check these specific items because each is mentioned as contributing to or being part of the investigation [Data: Entities (380, 381, 382, 386); Relationships (625, 626, 628, 630, 631)]. Medium-term controls: enforce or audit notification-related settings via endpoint management (e.g., Intune/GPO) where business policy requires consistent notification delivery; implement a detection rule or helpdesk alert to flag recent update events on endpoints that subsequently report lost notifications. For escalation/runbook, assign primary owner to desktop/endpoint support, with clear handoff to OS/update engineering if an update-induced configuration change is suspected. These recommendations are operational best practices inferred from the observed contributing factors; the dataset documents those factors and their relationships to the affected laptop [Data: Entities (383, 386); Relationships (630, 631)].

context: REPORT 56: # Management Reporting & Financial Reporting Infrastructure Standard (D365) — Customer Company 11 An approved operational and architecture standard (Management Reporting & Financial Reporting Infrastructure Standard V1.0) defines the design, security configuration and operational procedures for the Financial Reporting stack used in Customer Company 11's D365 Finance & Operations environments (UAT and PROD). The standard names a small set of high-impact components and dependencies: the Financial Reporting Data Mart (aggregated SQL store), the SQL backend that hosts metadata, an IIS/TLS layer that enforces cipher suites, service accounts used by reporting components, and operational actions such as Data Mart resets and decision-tree guided troubleshooting. The decision-tree/use-cases section maps observable symptoms (for example, reports showing authors as “unknown”) to concrete checks (service account connectivity, TLS/cipher-suite compatibility, SQL connectivity, AOS service, IIS configuration) and prescribes synchronous, environment-consistent changes (for cipher suites, AOS restarts, and Data Mart resets) to avoid broken flows across reporting components. The document is specifically authored for Customer Company 11 and is the authoritative reference tying troubleshooting, configuration management and recovery actions together. ## Standard document is the central governance artifact for reporting infrastructure The Management Reporting & Financial Reporting Infrastructure Standard V1.0 is the controlling architecture and operational standard for the Financial Reporting components in Customer Company 11's D365 environment and explicitly applies to UAT and PROD environments, making it the authoritative source for configuration and runbook behavior [Data: Entities (476); Relationships (778)]. Because the decision-tree and use-case sections are embedded in the same document, the standard both prescribes expected runtime configuration (TLS/cipher-suite rules, service-account behavior) and enumerates the troubleshooting workflow that engineers must follow when incidents occur, tying governance to operations [Data: Entities (476, 484); Relationships (784)]. ## Financial Reporting Data Mart is a single, critical dependency for report availability and correctness The Financial Reporting Data Mart is described as an aggregated SQL-based store relied upon by the Report Designer and Financial Reporting Service; its consistency is essential to correct report results and availability, which makes it a primary dependency in incident investigations [Data: Entities (478); Relationships (792)]. Operational guidance in the standard endorses a Data Mart reset as a remediation when data inconsistency is isolated to a single environment, indicating that the Data Mart is both a frequent root-cause locus and a recoverable subsystem when handled according to the runbook [Data: Entities (489); Relationships (795)]. ## SQL backend is the metadata authority and first-line troubleshooting target Report metadata and authorship information are stored and retrieved via the SQL backend/Data Mart; therefore SQL connectivity and database health directly affect metadata visibility and report rendering [Data: Entities (482, 485); Relationships (807)]. The decision-tree explicitly includes SQL connection checks as a primary step when reports or data are unavailable, making SQL health an early and repeatable check in incident triage workflows [Data: Entities (484, 482); Relationships (806)]. ## IIS/TLS layer and cipher-suite configuration are high-risk failure points for authentication and metadata issues An IIS-based TLS termination layer enforces TLS and cipher-suite settings for the reporting infrastructure; the standard highlights that misconfiguration here can block or degrade report access and functionality by breaking TLS negotiation between clients and backend components [Data: Entities (479, 486); Relationships (800, 802)]. The decision tree links TLS/cipher-suite incompatibility to observable symptoms such as report authors shown as “unknown,” indicating that TLS failures can manifest as metadata/authentication failures rather than obvious transport-level errors, and therefore must be included in runbooks and monitoring thresholds [Data: Relationships (804); Entities (491, 486)]. ## Service account connectivity and configuration are primary causes of missing author metadata The standard calls out service accounts as the identities used by reporting components to access the Data Mart and metadata; when report authors appear as “unknown,” the runbook specifically prescribes checking service account connectivity and permissions as a primary diagnostic action [Data: Entities (481, 491); Relationships (792, 805)]. This linkage makes service-account lifecycle controls (password/credential rotation, consistent configuration across environments) and monitoring of account authentication health operational priorities to avoid metadata loss and degraded author attribution [Data: Entities (481); Relationships (805)]. ## Decision tree maps symptoms to concrete component checks and environment-consistent change rules The document section containing decision trees and use cases maps specific symptoms to component checks (Data Mart reset, SQL connection, AOS service, IIS configuration, cipher-suite/TLS checks), providing an actionable troubleshooting workflow that ties observable issues to discrete remediation steps [Data: Entities (484); Relationships (784, 806)]. The standard further prescribes operational principles such as synchronizing cipher-suite changes across environments and performing AOS restarts after certain adjustments, which reduce the risk of asymmetric changes causing environment-specific failures; these procedural constraints should be enforced by change control and deployment automation [Data: Entities (476); Relationships (784)]. ## Recurring failure mode pattern: TLS/cipher mismatches and service-account authentication produce metadata anomalies The provided artifacts explicitly link TLS/cipher-suite incompatibility to both transport-level failures and the symptom of unknown report authors, and they separately identify service-account authentication problems as a cause of the same symptom, demonstrating a common failure-mode cluster where authentication/crypto misconfiguration and identity issues produce indistinct metadata anomalies [Data: Relationships (804, 805); Entities (486, 481, 491)]. Because multiple components (IIS/TLS layer, SQL backend/Data Mart, service accounts) can manifest the same operational symptom, the decision-tree's ordered checks (service accounts, TLS, SQL, Data Mart reset) should be codified into runbooks and automated diagnostics to avoid repeated firefighting and to direct engineers to the most-likely root cause quickly [Data: Entities (484, 478, 482); Relationships (792, 806, 795)]. ## Operational recovery guidance exists but relies on environment consistency and synchronous changes The standard contains explicit instructions that certain configuration changes (for example, allowed cipher suites) be synchronized across environments and that AOS restarts and Data Mart resets be used in a controlled manner; this indicates that successful recovery depends on disciplined change management and environment parity [Data: Entities (476, 486, 489); Relationships (784, 795)]. Absent automated enforcement of environment parity and pre-change compatibility checks, there is risk that asynchronous or partial rollouts (e.g., different cipher suites in UAT vs PROD) will create intermittent, environment-specific failures; the document's direction therefore implies the need for gating changes through the standard's decision-tree and CI/CD controls [Data: Entities (476, 486); Relationships (784)]. ## Controls and monitoring gaps implied by the document should be prioritized for remediation The artifact set highlights the criticality of TLS/cipher configuration, SQL/Data Mart health, and service-account authentication but does not itself include explicit monitoring/alert thresholds or automated remediation steps in the extracted records; given these components' operational impact, establishing targeted alerts for TLS negotiation failures, SQL connectivity errors, service-account authentication failures, and Data Mart consistency checks should be a near-term priority [Data: Entities (479, 482, 481, 478); Relationships (800, 806, 792, 795)]. Formalizing these checks into runbooks and integrating them with the decision-tree/use-cases in the standard (for example, automated pre-flight checks before cipher-suite changes and automated Data Mart consistency verification) will reduce time-to-detect and time-to-recover for the recurring failure modes documented here [Data: Entities (484, 476); Relationships (784)]. REPORT 25: # NDES / AD CS Certificate Enrollment Community (NDES, Issuing CA, Templates, Devices) This community centers on the Network Device Enrollment Service (NDES) as the operational bridge that automates certificate issuance from Active Directory Certificate Services (AD CS) to target devices (network appliances and managed endpoints). NDES depends directly on AD CS (Issuing CA and certificate templates) and on AD CS post-configuration steps being completed; it also integrates with Intune and targets both network devices and managed endpoints for automated enrollment. The Issuing CA and Certificate Templates are critical configuration points: misconfigured templates or incorrect CA selection commonly cause issuance failures, while Root CA and Issuing CA operational problems (expiry or mismanagement) break certificate validation across consumers. Key operational failure modes called out in the corpus include missing IIS components or NDES service account misconfiguration, incomplete AD CS post-configuration, and template/CA misconfiguration; the decision-tree used for troubleshooting explicitly gates issuance testing on AD CS post-configuration and template correctness. [Data: Entities (360, 364, 365, 370, 369, 375, 376, 363), Relationships (584, 589, 590, 592, 594, 595, 601, 602)] ## NDES is the central operational component connecting AD CS to device consumers NDES functions as the active enrollment service that requests certificates from AD CS and provisions them to devices and managed endpoints; this makes it the focal point for certificate-based device authentication in the environment [Data: Entities (360), Relationships (584, 590, 595)]. Because NDES both initiates requests (to the Issuing CA using configured templates) and delivers certificates to targets (network devices and managed endpoints), failures in NDES manifest as downstream authentication and connectivity problems for many device types—making timely detection and remediation critical for device management and network security posture [Data: Relationships (589, 594, 595)]. ## Common NDES failure modes are documented and actionable The NDES description explicitly lists operational failure modes including inability to start the service, missing IIS components, service-account misconfiguration, AD CS post-configuration errors, and broken communication between NDES and the CA; these are primary troubleshooting paths to investigate when issuance fails [Data: Entities (360)]. The decision-tree logic in the blueprint enforces verification steps (for example, checking AD CS post-configuration) before attempting issuance tests, which both documents these frequent failure causes and provides an operational sequence for root-cause isolation [Data: Entities (360), Relationships (592)]. ## Certificate template misconfiguration is a frequent, high-impact root cause Certificate templates define key usage, validity, and subject fields for issued certificates and are explicitly called out as a common cause of failed automated issuance when misconfigured; the blueprint recommends documentation and regular review of templates to prevent these issues [Data: Entities (365)]. Operationally, templates are consumed by the Issuing CA to produce end-entity certificates; if templates are incorrect on the CA side or selected incorrectly by NDES, issuance will fail or produce certificates incompatible with device expectations, causing authentication errors across the environment [Data: Relationships (602, 589)]. ## CA hierarchy (Root CA → Issuing CA) is a single point of trust and failure The Root CA is the trust anchor that signs the Issuing CA certificate; therefore issues at the Root CA or Issuing CA (expiry, mismanagement, incorrect chaining) can break certificate validation for all issued certificates and impact broad sets of devices and services [Data: Entities (363, 364), Relationships (601)]. Because NDES must be configured to use the correct Issuing CA, any mismatch between NDES configuration and the operational Issuing CA (or problems with the CA chain) will prevent successful enrollment and cause widespread authentication failures until corrected [Data: Relationships (584, 602)]. ## Network devices and managed endpoints are critical targets and verification points The blueprint identifies network appliances (routers, switches, firewalls, VPN gateways) and Intune-managed endpoints as primary recipients of NDES-issued certificates; these device classes should be part of any enrollment verification and post-change validation tests [Data: Entities (375, 376, 370), Relationships (594, 595, 590)]. Because successful issuance is verified by testing enrollment on targeted devices as the final decision-tree step, operational runbooks and monitoring should include sample enrollments against representative device models and managed endpoint profiles to detect regressions early [Data: Relationships (590)]. ## AD CS post-configuration is a gating dependency for NDES operation The corpus explicitly states that AD CS post-configuration (registration of NDES components, enabling required templates, selecting the correct Issuing CA) must be completed before proceeding to issuance tests; the decision tree checks this as a distinct step, making it a documented dependency that must be verified during deployments and troubleshooting [Data: Entities (369), Relationships (592)]. Operational processes should therefore include a checklist for AD CS post-configuration sign-off as a prerequisite for NDES activation and for any change that affects templates or CA selection to avoid wasted troubleshooting effort and false negatives during enrollment testing [Data: Entities (369), Relationships (592)]. ## Recommended operational controls: template governance, CA lifecycle management, and NDES platform hardening The dataset highlights template documentation and regular review as mitigations against issuance failures; implementing template governance (versioning, approvals, change logging) directly addresses a repeatedly-cited root cause [Data: Entities (365)]. Similarly, because Root CA and Issuing CA issues break validation widely, organizations need CA lifecycle controls (expiry monitoring, dedicated owners, offline Root CA handling procedures) to reduce systemic risk; these controls are supported by the Root CA and Issuing CA descriptions that call out expiry and mismanagement as service-impacting events [Data: Entities (363, 364), Relationships (601)]. Finally, hardening and operational checks for NDES—ensuring correct IIS components, validating the NDES service account, and maintaining reliable connectivity between NDES and the Issuing CA—map directly to the NDES failure modes and should be codified in installation and runbook procedures [Data: Entities (360)]. ## Suggested stakeholder/escalation pathway derived from dependency graph The relationship map shows NDES depending on AD CS (Issuing CA and templates) and provisioning certificates to devices, which implies a practical escalation sequence: NDES operations (first responder) → Issuing CA/AD CS administrators (certificate/template investigations) → Root CA owners (if trust-chain/expiry issues) → device owners (network or endpoint teams) for remediation and verification [Data: Entities (360, 364, 363, 370), Relationships (584, 601, 590)]. Making this path explicit in runbooks will shorten mean-time-to-resolution by directing incidents to the correct technical owner quickly—particularly for issues like template misconfiguration or CA chain problems that require CA-side changes rather than device-side fixes [Data: Relationships (589, 602, 590)].
